          Programming note: Money Stuff will be off for the rest of the wee=
k, back on Monday. Happy Thanksgiving!
Another note: This is being published a little before 1 p.m.=C2=A0New York =
time on Tuesday, Nov. 21. The OpenAI stuff moves pretty fast, and it is pos=
sible that by later this afternoon everything will be different. I mean, so=
rry?

 =20
   =20
      OpenAI: Nonprofit governance
   =20
 =20

One way to look at   the OpenAI situation is that=C2=A0OpenAI is a nonprofi=
t organization, and it is not that uncommon for nonprofits to have tension =
between their=C2=A0mission=C2=A0and their=C2=A0staff.
This is arguably a silly=C2=A0way to look at the situation, because, for a =
few years ending=C2=A0last Friday, nobody really thought of OpenAI as a non=
profit. OpenAI was an $86 billion tech startup that was building artificial=
 intelligence tools that were expected to=C2=A0result in huge profits for i=
ts investors (Microsoft Corp., venture capital firms) and employees (many o=
f whom owned stock). But technically=C2=A0that=C2=A0OpenAI =E2=80=94 OpenAI=
 Global LLC, the $86 billion startup with employee and VC and strategic sha=
reholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0was a subsidiary controlled by the nonprofit, Open=
AI Inc., and the nonprofit asserted itself dramatically last Friday when it=
s board of directors fired its chief executive officer, Sam Altman, and thr=
ew everything into chaos.
But for a moment ignore all of that and just think about OpenAI Inc., the 5=
01(c)(3) public charity,  with a mission of =E2=80=9Cbuilding safe and bene=
ficial artificial general intelligence for the benefit of humanity.=E2=80=
=9D Like any nonprofit, it has a mission that is described in its governing=
=C2=A0documents, and a board of directors who supervise the nonprofit to ma=
ke sure it is pursuing=C2=A0that mission, and a staff that it hires to achi=
eve the mission. The staff answers to the board, and the board answers to =
=E2=80=A6=C2=A0no one? Their own consciences? There are no shareholders; th=
e board=E2=80=99s main duties=C2=A0are to the mission.
Often, as a general matter, a nonprofit=E2=80=99s staff will be more commit=
ted to the mission than the board is. This just makes sense: The staff gene=
rally works full-time at the nonprofit,=C2=A0doing=C2=A0its mission all day=
; the directors are normally fancy outsiders with other jobs who just show =
up for occasional board meetings. Of course the staff cares more than the b=
oard does.
But it isn=E2=80=99t always quite that simple. Because the staff works full=
-time at the nonprofit, they will care much more about the practical condit=
ions of the job than the board will. The board is disinterested and comfort=
able and can care entirely about the abstract mission of the nonprofit; the=
 staff members have to pay rent and student loans. And so sometimes there w=
ill be a conflict between the=C2=A0mission=C2=A0of the nonprofit and the=C2=
=A0conditions of the job, and the staff will prefer better working conditio=
ns while the board will prefer the mission.
  [1]

So a charity to feed the homeless might have to decide whether to spend a m=
arginal dollar of donations on food for the homeless or higher salaries for=
 the staff. It is not=C2=A0obvious=C2=A0that the staff will prefer higher s=
alaries while the board will prefer feeding more clients, but it is=C2=A0po=
ssible; really it is a pretty standard story of agency costs, and the board=
=E2=80=99s role is to manage=C2=A0those costs.=C2=A0Or last year  Ryan Grim=
 wrote=C2=A0about conflicts within progressive advocacy groups after the ki=
lling of George Floyd: =E2=80=9CIn the eyes of group leaders=C2=A0 ...=C2=
=A0staff were ignoring the mission and focusing only on themselves, using a=
 moment of public awakening to smuggle through standard grievances cloaked =
in the language of social justice,=E2=80=9D while the staff =E2=80=9Cbeliev=
ed [that]=C2=A0managers exploited the moral commitment staff felt toward th=
eir mission, allowing workplace abuses to go unchecked.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
OpenAI is a very strange nonprofit! Its stated mission is =E2=80=9Cbuilding=
 safe and beneficial artificial general intelligence for the benefit of hum=
anity,=E2=80=9D but in the unavoidably sci-fi world of artificial intellige=
nce developers, that mission=C2=A0has a bit of a flavor of =E2=80=9Cbuildin=
g artificial intelligence very very carefully and being ready to shut it do=
wn at any point if it looks likely to go rogue and kill all of humanity.=E2=
=80=9D The mission is =E2=80=9Cbuild AI, but not too much of it, or too qui=
ckly, or too commercially.=E2=80=9D As of last week, it had a board with si=
x members, three of whom (including Altman) worked at OpenAI and  three of =
whom did not.
And it is easy to see how the board=E2=80=99s view of the mission could con=
flict with the staff=E2=80=99s views of their jobs.=C2=A0Like, you are a cu=
tting-edge AI researcher, you come into work every day excited to do cuttin=
g-edge AI research, you=C2=A0succeed=C2=A0in doing cutting-edge stuff, and =
the board shows up and is like =E2=80=9Chey this edge is too cutting, we wo=
rry it=E2=80=99s going to kill us all, slow it down there tiger.=E2=80=9D I=
t=E2=80=99s condescending! It stops you from doing the thing that you are c=
ommitted to do! They=E2=80=99re Luddites! But the thing that you are commit=
ted to do (build cutting-edge AI stuff)=C2=A0is not=C2=A0quite=C2=A0the thi=
ng that OpenAI is committed to do (build safe AI stuff). And the outside di=
rectors =E2=80=94 who=C2=A0don=E2=80=99t go to work at OpenAI all day=C2=A0=
=E2=80=94=C2=A0might care more about its official mission than the staff do=
es.
From the board=E2=80=99s perspective, a nonprofit with the mission of =E2=
=80=9Cbe first to build artificial general intelligence, but only if we can=
 do it safely=E2=80=9D will have a staffing problem. To achieve that missio=
n it will have to hire staff who are talented and driven enough to be the f=
irst to build AGI, but those staff will probably be more enthusiastic about=
 AI, generally, than the mission calls for. Or you can hire staff who are s=
uper-nervous about AGI, but they probably won=E2=80=99t be the first ones t=
o build it. So you hire the good AI developers, but you keep a watchful eye=
 on them.
From the staff=E2=80=99s perspective, the board is a bunch of outsiders who=
se main features are (1) they are worried about AI safety and (2) they don=
=E2=80=99t work at OpenAI. (Well, three of them do, but three =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0a majority of those who voted to oust Altman =E2=80=94=C2=A0don=E2=80=99=
t.)=C2=A0They have no idea!=C2=A0They are meddling in stuff =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
AI research but also intra-company dynamics =E2=80=94=C2=A0that they don=E2=
=80=99t really understand, driven by an=C2=A0abstract sense of mission. Whi=
ch kind of=C2=A0is the=C2=A0job of a nonprofit board, but which will reason=
ably annoy the staff.
Also, of course, the material conditions of the OpenAI staff are pretty unu=
sual for a nonprofit: They can get paid=C2=A0millions of dollars a year and=
 they own equity in the for-profit subsidiary, equity that they were about =
to be able to   sell at an $86 billion valuation. When the board is like =
=E2=80=9Cno, the mission requires us to zero your equity and cut off our ow=
n future funding,=E2=80=9D I mean, maybe that is very noble and mission-dri=
ven of the board. But, just economically, it is rough on the staff.
Yesterday virtually all of OpenAI=E2=80=99s staff  signed an open letter to=
 the board, demanding that the board resign and bring back Altman. The lett=
er claims that the board =E2=80=9Cinformed the leadership team that allowin=
g the company to be destroyed =E2=80=98would be consistent with the mission=
.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D Yes! I mean, the board might be wrong about the facts, =
but=C2=A0in principle=C2=A0it is absolutely possible that destroying OpenAI=
=E2=80=99s business would be consistent with its mission. If you have built=
 an unsafe AI, you delete the code and burn down the building. The mission =
is conditional =E2=80=94=C2=A0build AGI if it is safe =E2=80=94=C2=A0and if=
 the condition is not satisfied then you go ahead and destroy all of the wo=
rk.=C2=A0That is the board=E2=80=99s job. It=E2=80=99s the board=E2=80=99s =
job because it can=E2=80=99t be the staff=E2=80=99s job, because the staff =
is there to do the work, and will be too conflicted to destroy it. The boar=
d is there to supervise the mission.
I don=E2=80=99t mean to say that the board is right! The board really are o=
utside kibbitzers! Between OpenAI=E2=80=99s staff, who know what they=E2=80=
=99re talking about but also kinda like building AI, and OpenAI=E2=80=99s b=
oard, who lean more to being AI-skeptical outsiders, I guess=C2=A0I=E2=80=
=99d bet on the staff being right.
  [2]
 (Also if the board=E2=80=99s job is to prevent the development of rogue AI=
, burning down OpenAI is unlikely to accomplish that, just because there ar=
e competitors who will gleefully hire the staff.) I am just saying that thi=
s is a standard and real problem in nonprofit governance, and what=E2=80=99=
s weird about OpenAI is that it=E2=80=99s an $86 billion startup with nonpr=
ofit governance.
I guess the other thing to say is that, generally speaking, a staff is ofte=
n=C2=A0more essential to a nonprofit than a board is? (Except that at a lot=
 of nonprofits =E2=80=94=C2=A0not OpenAI! =E2=80=94=C2=A0the directors tend=
 to also be big donors and fundraisers.) Like, the staff does the work; the=
 board just goes to occasional meetings. If the staff all quit then the non=
profit is in trouble; if the directors=C2=A0all quit they=E2=80=99re pretty=
 replaceable. As of last night here=E2=80=99s the state of things, from   B=
loomberg=E2=80=99s=C2=A0Shirin Ghaffary:

OpenAI said it=E2=80=99s in =E2=80=9Cintense discussions=E2=80=9D to unify =
the company after another tumultuous day that saw most employees threaten t=
o quit if Sam Altman doesn=E2=80=99t return as chief executive officer.
Vice President of Global Affairs Anna Makanju delivered the message in an i=
nternal memo reviewed by Bloomberg News, aiming to rally staff who=E2=80=99=
ve grown anxious after days of disarray following Altman=E2=80=99s ouster a=
nd the board=E2=80=99s surprise appointment of former Twitch chief Emmett S=
hear as his interim replacement.
OpenAI management is in touch with Altman, Shear and the board =E2=80=9Cbut=
 they are not prepared to give us a final response this evening,=E2=80=9D M=
akanju wrote. =E2=80=A6
There=E2=80=99s strong momentum outside OpenAI to get Altman reinstated too=
. OpenAI=E2=80=99s other investors, led by Thrive Capital, are actively try=
ing to orchestrate his return, people with knowledge of the effort told Blo=
omberg News Monday. Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella told Emily Chang in a Bloom=
berg Television interview that even he wouldn=E2=80=99t oppose Altman=E2=80=
=99s reinstatement. ...
=E2=80=9CWe are continuing to go over mutually acceptable options and are s=
cheduled to speak again tomorrow morning when everyone=E2=80=99s had a litt=
le more sleep,=E2=80=9D Makanju wrote. =E2=80=9CThese intense discussions c=
an drag out, and I know it can feel impossible to be patient.=E2=80=9D

   =20
      OpenAI: Startup governance
   =20
 =20

Obviously another way to look at the OpenAI situation is that OpenAI is an =
$86 billion tech startup that did some real odd stuff to incinerate most of=
 its value.=C2=A0
In some ways this is not=C2=A0that=C2=A0unusual a story of nonprofit govern=
ance, in which the board=E2=80=99s abstract commitment to the mission confl=
icts with the practical on-the-ground experiences of the staff. But it sure=
 is an unusual story of startup governance, in which Microsoft agreed to in=
vest $13 billion in OpenAI, and other venture capitalists also put in money=
, and employees got stock grants, without the contractual or fiduciary righ=
ts that investors would normally get. And then one day the board of OpenAI =
was like =E2=80=9Chey we decided to blow up the company=E2=80=9D and the in=
vestors were like =E2=80=9Cwait a minute can you really do that=E2=80=9D an=
d the board was like =E2=80=9Coh yeah sure we can.=E2=80=9D So   Bloomberg =
reports=C2=A0that =E2=80=9Csome investors were considering writing down the=
 value of their OpenAI holdings to zero.=E2=80=9D Eighty-six billion dollar=
s of value evaporated in a weekend.
One popular thing to say about this is that the investors should have been =
more careful about governance, and that future investors in future startups=
 will pay more attention to things like control rights and fiduciary duties=
 and board composition. And, maybe. But I have to say I sympathize with the=
 investors here. There are=C2=A0many cases in which sophisticated investors=
 invest large sums of money into companies where they have no real control =
rights, and they=C2=A0rationally=C2=A0calculate that it will be fine. Gener=
ally the calculation will involve some combination of factors like:
=09I have met the founder and shook her hand and looked into her eyes and I=
 trust her, so I do not need to care about the corporate formalities. (Smar=
t investors jumped into   Elon Musk=E2=80=99s Twitter Inc. adventure not be=
cause they did extensive due diligence or got a lot of control rights, but =
because he=E2=80=99s Elon Musk.)=09Regardless of the formalities, the=C2=A0=
incentives=C2=A0are on my side: This company will need more money, the foun=
der will need to sell her shares, and so even if she has the formal right t=
o hose me she won=E2=80=99t, because that will be bad for her. (Both Adam N=
eumann and Travis Kalanick were forced out of startups that they had founde=
d and where they had more or less total formal control, because their inves=
tors told them =E2=80=9Chey look if you keep your total control this is goi=
ng to be a zero, whereas if you leave now you can salvage some value for yo=
urself,=E2=80=9D and they made a rational choice.)=09The formalities are ba=
d, sure, but that=E2=80=99s the price of getting into this investment, and =
the upside of this investment is so huge that I am willing to take the risk=
 of getting hosed by bad governance. (Early investors in Facebook Inc., now=
 Meta Platforms Inc.,=C2=A0had very little in the way of governance rights,=
 Mark Zuckerberg had total control, and guess what he still does and he has=
 made those investors very rich.)

It is not hard to see how OpenAI=E2=80=99s investors could have had similar=
 thoughts:
=09They really liked Sam Altman! He is a popular and well-connected figure =
among venture capital types. Nor, really, were they wrong to trust him. It=
=E2=80=99s just that usually startup founders have much more control of the=
ir boards than Altman did. That=C2=A0did=C2=A0turn out to be a failure of o=
rganizational due diligence by OpenAI=E2=80=99s investors, but an understan=
dable one.=09The incentives were just incredibly on their side. OpenAI requ=
ires piles and piles of outside money to do its work, so it cannot rational=
ly afford to alienate investors. Microsoft, OpenAI=E2=80=99s biggest invest=
or, also provides its computing power and has a license to its technology a=
nd, after this weekend=E2=80=99s implosion, seems to be on track to hire mo=
st of its staff. =E2=80=9CYou can make the case that Microsoft just acquire=
d OpenAI for $0 and zero risk of an antitrust lawsuit,=E2=80=9D  Ben Thomps=
on wrote yesterday. No rational startup would let that happen! Meanwhile Th=
rive Capital was  leading the tender offer to buy employee shares, and migh=
t=C2=A0have thought =E2=80=9Cthese employees need liquidity and will not bi=
te the hand that is feeding them.=E2=80=9D These were all, I think, very re=
asonable things to think; they were just flummoxed by a board that=C2=A0did=
 not act in the economic best interests of the company. Again: because it w=
asn=E2=80=99t supposed to! Still a jarring surprise.=09The upside was reall=
y big. I mean the company was worth more than $80 billion last week, not be=
cause it was profitable (it was a money pit) but because, you know, it had =
an   8% chance of being a trillion-dollar company. You=E2=80=99d take some =
governance risk for that upside.

I feel like the lesson here is not so much =E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99t invest in=
 startups without vetting the board and ideally getting a board seat for yo=
urself,=E2=80=9D and more =E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99t invest in nonprofits at an=
 $86 billion valuation.=E2=80=9D Which I think has never come up before? Li=
ke as far as I can tell no one in human history has ever purchased shares i=
n a nonprofit at an $86 billion valuation? Because purchasing shares in a n=
onprofit, at any valuation, is not a coherent thing to do? But then OpenAI=
=C2=A0made it happen, for the first time, and probably also the last.
There are other oddities here that I do not really understand but want to m=
ention as puzzles. Like, the status as of this morning really does seem to =
be that Sam Altman has been fired by OpenAI, that he plans to go work at Mi=
crosoft (OpenAI=E2=80=99s biggest investor and partner), and that he is goi=
ng to take the large majority of OpenAI=E2=80=99s employees with him. What =
=E2=80=A6=C2=A0what do=C2=A0everyone=E2=80=99s contracts look like here? Li=
ke:
=09When Microsoft signed its deal with OpenAI, did it agree to any sort of =
non-compete or non-solicit, like, =E2=80=9Cyou won=E2=80=99t hire away our =
employees=E2=80=9D?=09Does Altman have any sort of non-solicit, like, =E2=
=80=9Cif you leave you won=E2=80=99t hire everyone else=E2=80=9D?=09Do the =
employees have any sort of non-disclosure agreements, like, =E2=80=9Cif you=
 leave you won=E2=80=99t take our proprietary technology with you=E2=80=9D?=
=C2=A0Perhaps this one doesn=E2=80=99t matter, since Microsoft has a licens=
e to OpenAI=E2=80=99s intellectual property: The OpenAI employees can leave=
, take nothing with them, and then go work at Microsoft and have access to =
everything they left behind. But Bloomberg=E2=80=99s   Austin Carr reports =
that =E2=80=9Cany employees who do join Microsoft can=E2=80=99t simply repl=
icate the work they were doing on OpenAI properties like GPT-5 without invi=
ting a nightmare of claims over trade-secret theft.=E2=80=9D

Coming from the world of finance, all of this feels odd to me; ordinarily t=
here would be contracts preventing a company=E2=80=99s biggest customer fro=
m hiring its CEO and him then bringing over his whole team to build the sam=
e cutting-edge technology they were building at the company. Here, I guess,=
 there aren=E2=80=99t? Everyone just trusted each other? Seems like a mista=
ke.

      Binance
   =20
 =20

Broadly speaking the US government has two objections to Binance Holdings L=
td.:
=09Binance is a crypto exchange, and the US Securities and Exchange Commiss=
ion thinks that crypto exchanges are more or less illegal under US law.
  [3]

=09Binance, as a crypto exchange, provides a way for people to move money i=
nternationally, and the US Department of Justice thinks that Binance lets=
=C2=A0bad=C2=A0people move=C2=A0bad=C2=A0money to and from bad countries.

We have   talked about the first objection before, because the SEC has actu=
ally sued Binance and laid out its objections. But it is, I think,=C2=A0by =
far=C2=A0the less=C2=A0serious objection, for several reasons:
=09The SEC might be wrong on the law? There is very much a live controversy=
 about whether US securities regulation applies to crypto as broadly as the=
 SEC thinks it does, and other crypto firms have fought the SEC in court an=
d   sometimes even won. Whereas if the Justice Department is like =E2=80=9C=
you laundered money for criminals and violated US sanctions,=E2=80=9D look,=
 they=C2=A0might=C2=A0be wrong, but you are going to have a much harder tim=
e fighting that in court.=09Even if the SEC is right, there is a fairly sim=
ple solution: Stop letting US customers trade on Binance.
  [4]
=C2=A0It is broadly illegal to do securities stuff with US customers withou=
t following SEC rules, but it cannot actually be illegal under US law for a=
 foreign company to allow foreign customers to trade foreign securities. Wh=
ereas it=C2=A0kind of is illegal under US law for a foreign company to allo=
w foreign customers to send money to Iran.
  [5]
 If you operate a crypto exchange with absolutely no US customers at all, b=
ut you let terrorist organizations move money on it, the US is going to car=
e. You can ring-fence yourself from the US and solve your securities-law pr=
oblems, but that doesn=E2=80=99t work for your money-laundering or sanction=
s problems.=09The SEC can=E2=80=99t put you in jail, but the Justice Depart=
ment can.

Now, I don=E2=80=99t know exactly what=C2=A0the Justice Department=E2=80=99=
s concerns with Binance are, because I have not yet seen its complaint.
  [6]
 But just as a structural matter you can tell that (1) they=E2=80=99re seri=
ous and (2) Binance is taking them seriously. So Bloomberg=E2=80=99s=C2=A0C=
hris Strohm, Allyson Versprille=C2=A0and Olga Kharif   reported yesterday:

The US Justice Department is seeking more than $4 billion from Binance Hold=
ings Ltd. as part of a proposed resolution of a years-long investigation in=
to the world=E2=80=99s largest cryptocurrency exchange.
Negotiations between the Justice Department and Binance include the possibi=
lity that its founder Changpeng Zhao would face criminal charges in the US =
under an agreement to resolve the probe into alleged money laundering, bank=
 fraud and sanctions violations, according to people familiar with the disc=
ussions.
Zhao, also known as =E2=80=9CCZ,=E2=80=9D is residing in the United Arab Em=
irates, which doesn=E2=80=99t have an extradition treaty with the US, but t=
hat doesn=E2=80=99t prevent him from coming voluntarily. =E2=80=A6
With respect to possible sanctions violations, the Justice Department has b=
een investigating Binance for allegedly aiding in the evasion of US sanctio=
ns against Iran and Russia, one of the people said. Binance has also been u=
nder scrutiny for whether it allowed transactions that helped finance Hamas=
.

I don=E2=80=99t give legal advice around here but my general view is that i=
f you are a foreigner living abroad and the US government is like =E2=80=9C=
why don=E2=80=99t you voluntarily come over here so we can put you in priso=
n for money laundering=E2=80=9D you should maybe=C2=A0not=C2=A0volunteer fo=
r that? But what do I know; the Wall Street Journal  just reported:

The chief executive of Binance, the largest global cryptocurrency exchange,=
 plans to step down and plead guilty to violating criminal U.S. anti-money-=
laundering requirements, in a deal that may preserve the company=E2=80=99s =
ability to continue operating, according to people familiar with the matter=
.=C2=A0
Changpeng Zhao is scheduled to appear in Seattle federal court Tuesday afte=
rnoon and enter his plea, the people said. Binance, which Zhao owns, will a=
lso plead guilty to a criminal charge and agree to pay fines totaling $4.3 =
billion, which includes amounts to settle civil allegations made by regulat=
ors, the people said. ...
The deal would allow Zhao to retain his majority ownership of Binance, alth=
ough he won=E2=80=99t be able to have an executive role at the company. He =
would face sentencing at a later date.

      Ozempic is good/bad for business
   =20
 =20

On the one hand, Bloomberg=E2=80=99s   Deena Shanker reports that Ozempic i=
s the Grinch that stole Thanksgiving:

Millions of Americans are now taking appetite suppressing GLP-1 hormone inj=
ections, and for many this will be their first Thanksgiving on the drug.
Food manufacturers and retailers =E2=80=94 who normally enjoy robust sales =
during the holiday feasting season =E2=80=94 are bracing for an impact that=
 could extend far beyond one or two holidays. Walmart Inc. said in October =
that its shoppers taking weight-loss drugs were buying slightly less food. =
Steve Cahillane, the chief executive officer of Kellanova, maker of Pringle=
s and Cheez-Its, told Bloomberg the company was studying the impact on diet=
ary behaviors to be able to respond. Shares of Krispy Kreme Inc. were downg=
raded last month on concerns weight-loss drugs would reduce demand for thei=
r doughnuts.

On the other hand, Isabelle Lee reports that   there=E2=80=99s an ETF now:
A niche issuer is launching a biotechnology exchange-traded fund whose key =
holdings include firms benefiting from the hype around weight-loss drugs.
I suppose the trendy Thanksgiving trade is long weight-loss-drugs and short=
 food manufacturers.

  [1] I am contrasting the =E2=80=9Cstaff=E2=80=9D and the =E2=80=9Cboard,=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note: </strong><em>Money S=
tuff will be off for the rest of the week, back on Monday. Happy Thanksgivi=
ng!</em></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Another note:</strong><em> This is bei=
ng published a little before 1 p.m.=C2=A0New York time on Tuesday, Nov. 21.=
 The OpenAI stuff moves pretty fast, and it is possible that by later this =
afternoon everything will be different. I mean, sorry?</em></p>
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">OpenAI: Nonprofit governance</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One way to look at <a href=3D"https://link.mai=
l.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnL=
mNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMTEtMjAvd2hvLWNvbnRyb2xzLW9wZW5haT9jbXBp=
ZD1CQkQxMTIxMjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2x=
ldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzExMjEmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b85=
24a319a23cB45346ef4" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">  the OpenAI situation</a> is that=C2=A0OpenA=
I is a nonprofit organization, and it is not that uncommon for nonprofits t=
o have tension between their=C2=A0<em>mission=C2=A0</em>and their=C2=A0<em>=
staff</em>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is arguably a <em>silly=C2=A0</em>way to =
look at the situation, because, for a few years ending=C2=A0last Friday, no=
body really thought of OpenAI as a nonprofit. OpenAI was an $86 billion tec=
h startup that was building artificial intelligence tools that were expecte=
d to=C2=A0result in huge profits for its investors (Microsoft Corp., ventur=
e capital firms) and employees (many of whom owned stock). But technically=
=C2=A0<em>that=C2=A0</em>OpenAI =E2=80=94 OpenAI Global LLC, the $86 billio=
n startup with employee and VC and strategic shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0wa=
s a subsidiary controlled by the nonprofit, OpenAI Inc., and the nonprofit =
asserted itself dramatically last Friday when its board of directors fired =
its chief executive officer, Sam Altman, and threw everything into chaos.</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But for a moment ignore all of that and just t=
hink about OpenAI Inc., the 501(c)(3) public charity, <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly9vcGVuYWkuY29=
tL291ci1zdHJ1Y3R1cmU/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBb8558f31" itemprop=3D"StoryLi=
nk" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> with a mis=
sion of</a> =E2=80=9Cbuilding safe and beneficial artificial general intell=
igence for the benefit of humanity.=E2=80=9D Like any nonprofit, it has a m=
ission that is described in its governing=C2=A0documents, and a board of di=
rectors who supervise the nonprofit to make sure it is pursuing=C2=A0that m=
ission, and a staff that it hires to achieve the mission. The staff answers=
 to the board, and the board answers to =E2=80=A6=C2=A0no one? Their own co=
nsciences? There are no shareholders; the board=E2=80=99s main duties=C2=A0=
are to the mission.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Often, as a general matter, a nonprofit=E2=80=
=99s staff will be more committed to the mission than the board is. This ju=
st makes sense: The staff generally works full-time at the nonprofit,=C2=A0=
<em>doing=C2=A0</em>its mission all day; the directors are normally fancy o=
utsiders with other jobs who just show up for occasional board meetings. Of=
 course the staff cares more than the board does.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But it isn=E2=80=99t always quite that simple.=
 Because the staff works full-time at the nonprofit, they will care much mo=
re about the practical conditions of the job than the board will. The board=
 is disinterested and comfortable and can care entirely about the abstract =
mission of the nonprofit; the staff members have to pay rent and student lo=
ans. And so sometimes there will be a conflict between the=C2=A0<em>mission=
=C2=A0</em>of the nonprofit and the=C2=A0<em>conditions of the job</em>, an=
d the staff will prefer better working conditions while the board will pref=
er the mission.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; backgrou=
nd-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So a charity to feed the homeless might have t=
o decide whether to spend a marginal dollar of donations on food for the ho=
meless or higher salaries for the staff. It is not=C2=A0<em>obvious=C2=A0</=
em>that the staff will prefer higher salaries while the board will prefer f=
eeding more clients, but it is=C2=A0<em>possible</em>; really it is a prett=
y standard story of agency costs, and the board=E2=80=99s role is to manage=
=C2=A0those costs.=C2=A0Or last year <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly90aGVpbnRlcmNlcHQuY29tLzIwMjIv=
MDYvMTMvcHJvZ3Jlc3NpdmUtb3JnYW5pemluZy1pbmZpZ2h0aW5nLWNhbGxvdXQtY3VsdHVyZS8=
/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd886839a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"ite=
mscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !i=
mportant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bo=
ttom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Ryan Grim wrote=C2=A0about</a>=
 conflicts within progressive advocacy groups after the killing of George F=
loyd: =E2=80=9CIn the eyes of group leaders=C2=A0 ...=C2=A0staff were ignor=
ing the mission and focusing only on themselves, using a moment of public a=
wakening to smuggle through standard grievances cloaked in the language of =
social justice,=E2=80=9D while the staff =E2=80=9Cbelieved [that]=C2=A0mana=
gers exploited the moral commitment staff felt toward their mission, allowi=
ng workplace abuses to go unchecked.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">OpenAI is a very strange nonprofit! Its stated=
 mission is =E2=80=9Cbuilding safe and beneficial artificial general intell=
igence for the benefit of humanity,=E2=80=9D but in the unavoidably sci-fi =
world of artificial intelligence developers, that mission=C2=A0has a bit of=
 a flavor of =E2=80=9Cbuilding artificial intelligence very very carefully =
and being ready to shut it down at any point if it looks likely to go rogue=
 and kill all of humanity.=E2=80=9D The mission is =E2=80=9Cbuild AI, but n=
ot too much of it, or too quickly, or too commercially.=E2=80=9D As of last=
 week, it had a board with six members, three of whom (including Altman) wo=
rked at OpenAI and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=
/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS90ZWNoL2FpL29wZW5haS1ib2FyZC1zYW=
0tYWx0bWFuLWQ1ZjNjZDQ5/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB87fdfc55" itemprop=3D"Story=
Link" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> three of=
 whom did not</a>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And it is easy to see how the board=E2=80=99s =
view of the mission could conflict with the staff=E2=80=99s views of their =
jobs.=C2=A0Like, you are a cutting-edge AI researcher, you come into work e=
very day excited to do cutting-edge AI research, you=C2=A0<em>succeed=C2=A0=
</em>in doing cutting-edge stuff, and the board shows up and is like =E2=80=
=9Chey this edge is too cutting, we worry it=E2=80=99s going to kill us all=
, slow it down there tiger.=E2=80=9D It=E2=80=99s condescending! It stops y=
ou from doing the thing that you are committed to do! They=E2=80=99re Luddi=
tes! But the thing that you are committed to do (build cutting-edge AI stuf=
f)=C2=A0is not=C2=A0<em>quite=C2=A0</em>the thing that OpenAI is committed =
to do (build safe AI stuff). And the outside directors =E2=80=94 who=C2=A0<=
em>don=E2=80=99t go to work at OpenAI all day=C2=A0</em>=E2=80=94=C2=A0migh=
t care more about its official mission than the staff does.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">From the board=E2=80=99s perspective, a nonpro=
fit with the mission of =E2=80=9Cbe first to build artificial general intel=
ligence, but only if we can do it safely=E2=80=9D will have a staffing prob=
lem. To achieve that mission it will have to hire staff who are talented an=
d driven enough to be the first to build AGI, but those staff will probably=
 be more enthusiastic about AI, generally, than the mission calls for. Or y=
ou can hire staff who are super-nervous about AGI, but they probably won=E2=
=80=99t be the first ones to build it. So you hire the good AI developers, =
but you keep a watchful eye on them.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">From the staff=E2=80=99s perspective, the boar=
d is a bunch of outsiders whose main features are (1) they are worried abou=
t AI safety and (2) they don=E2=80=99t work at OpenAI. (Well, three of them=
 do, but three =E2=80=94=C2=A0a majority of those who voted to oust Altman =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0don=E2=80=99t.)=C2=A0<em>They have no idea!=C2=A0</em>They a=
re meddling in stuff =E2=80=94=C2=A0AI research but also intra-company dyna=
mics =E2=80=94=C2=A0that they don=E2=80=99t really understand, driven by an=
=C2=A0abstract sense of mission. Which kind of=C2=A0<em>is</em> the=C2=A0jo=
b of a nonprofit board, but which will reasonably annoy the staff.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also, of course, the material conditions of th=
e OpenAI staff are pretty unusual for a nonprofit: They can get paid=C2=A0<=
a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR=
0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS90ZWNoL29wZW5haS1lbXBsb3llZXMtdGhyZWF0ZW4tdG8tcXVpdC=
11bmxlc3MtYm9hcmQtcmVzaWducy1iYmQ1Y2M4Ng/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB49da0ca0"=
 itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"=
color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px;=
 border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color=
: #ccc;">millions of dollars a year</a> and they own equity in the for-prof=
it subsidiary, equity that they were about to be able to <a href=3D"https:/=
/link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxv=
b21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMTEtMjAvb3BlbmFpLWludmVzdG9ycy1sZWQ=
tYnktdGhyaXZlLWFuZ2xlLXRvLWJyaW5nLWJhY2stYWx0bWFuP2NtcGlkPUJCRDExMjEyM19NT0=
5FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtP=
TIzMTEyMSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB597f149b=
" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D=
"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px=
; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-colo=
r: #ccc;">  sell at an $86 billion valuation</a>. When the board is like =
=E2=80=9Cno, the mission requires us to zero your equity and cut off our ow=
n future funding,=E2=80=9D I mean, maybe that is very noble and mission-dri=
ven of the board. But, just economically, it is rough on the staff.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yesterday virtually all of OpenAI=E2=80=99s st=
aff <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33446549.25842=
5/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYXhpb3MuY29tLzIwMjMvMTEvMjAvb3BlbmFpLXN0YWZmLWxldHRlci1ib=
2FyZC1yZXNpZ24tc2FtLWFsdG1hbg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBfcd58db8" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
 signed an open letter to the board</a>, demanding that the board resign an=
d bring back Altman. The letter claims that the board =E2=80=9Cinformed the=
 leadership team that allowing the company to be destroyed =E2=80=98would b=
e consistent with the mission.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D Yes! I mean, the board mig=
ht be wrong about the facts, but=C2=A0<em>in principle=C2=A0</em>it is abso=
lutely possible that destroying OpenAI=E2=80=99s business would be consiste=
nt with its mission. If you have built an unsafe AI, you delete the code an=
d burn down the building. The mission is conditional =E2=80=94=C2=A0build A=
GI if it is safe =E2=80=94=C2=A0and if the condition is not satisfied then =
you go ahead and destroy all of the work.=C2=A0<em>That is the board=E2=80=
=99s job</em>. It=E2=80=99s the board=E2=80=99s job because it can=E2=80=99=
t be the staff=E2=80=99s job, because the staff is there to do the work, an=
d will be too conflicted to destroy it. The board is there to supervise the=
 mission.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t mean to say that the board is =
right! The board really are outside kibbitzers! Between OpenAI=E2=80=99s st=
aff, who know what they=E2=80=99re talking about but also kinda like buildi=
ng AI, and OpenAI=E2=80=99s board, who lean more to being AI-skeptical outs=
iders, I <em>guess=C2=A0</em>I=E2=80=99d bet on the staff being right.<a cl=
ass=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-d=
ecoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: =
#000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparen=
t !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a> (Also if the board=E2=80=99s job is to prevent the development of rogu=
e AI, burning down OpenAI is unlikely to accomplish that, just because ther=
e are competitors who will gleefully hire the staff.) I am just saying that=
 this is a standard and real problem in nonprofit governance, and what=E2=
=80=99s weird about OpenAI is that it=E2=80=99s an $86 billion startup with=
 nonprofit governance.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I guess the other thing to say is that, genera=
lly speaking, a staff is often=C2=A0more essential to a nonprofit than a bo=
ard is? (Except that at a lot of nonprofits =E2=80=94=C2=A0not OpenAI! =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0the directors tend to also be big donors and fundraisers.) Like=
, the staff does the work; the board just goes to occasional meetings. If t=
he staff all quit then the nonprofit is in trouble; if the directors=C2=A0a=
ll quit they=E2=80=99re pretty replaceable. As of last night here=E2=80=99s=
 the state of things, from <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=
om/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbG=
VzLzIwMjMtMTEtMjEvb3BlbmFpLWluLWludGVuc2UtZGlzY3Vzc2lvbnMtdG8tdW5pZnktY29tc=
GFueS1tZW1vLXNheXM_Y21waWQ9QkJEMTEyMTIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFp=
bCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMxMTIxJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25=
leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB6f6ac6a5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemsco=
pe=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration=
: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; =
border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  Bloomberg=E2=80=99s=
=C2=A0Shirin Ghaffary</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">OpenAI said it=E2=80=99s in =E2=80=9Cintense d=
iscussions=E2=80=9D to unify the company after another tumultuous day that =
saw most employees threaten to quit if Sam Altman doesn=E2=80=99t return as=
 chief executive officer.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Vice President of Global Affairs Anna Makanju =
delivered the message in an internal memo reviewed by Bloomberg News, aimin=
g to rally staff who=E2=80=99ve grown anxious after days of disarray follow=
ing Altman=E2=80=99s ouster and the board=E2=80=99s surprise appointment of=
 former Twitch chief Emmett Shear as his interim replacement.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">OpenAI management is in touch with Altman, She=
ar and the board =E2=80=9Cbut they are not prepared to give us a final resp=
onse this evening,=E2=80=9D Makanju wrote. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There=E2=80=99s strong momentum outside OpenAI=
 to get Altman reinstated too. OpenAI=E2=80=99s other investors, led by Thr=
ive Capital, are actively trying to orchestrate his return, people with kno=
wledge of the effort told Bloomberg News Monday. Microsoft CEO Satya Nadell=
a told Emily Chang in a Bloomberg Television interview that even he wouldn=
=E2=80=99t oppose Altman=E2=80=99s reinstatement. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWe are continuing to go over mutually=
 acceptable options and are scheduled to speak again tomorrow morning when =
everyone=E2=80=99s had a little more sleep,=E2=80=9D Makanju wrote. =E2=80=
=9CThese intense discussions can drag out, and I know it can feel impossibl=
e to be patient.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>

<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">OpenAI: Startup governance</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Obviously another way to look at the OpenAI si=
tuation is that OpenAI is an $86 billion tech startup that did some real od=
d stuff to incinerate most of its value.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In some ways this is not=C2=A0<em>that=C2=A0</=
em>unusual a story of nonprofit governance, in which the board=E2=80=99s ab=
stract commitment to the mission conflicts with the practical on-the-ground=
 experiences of the staff. But it sure is an unusual story of startup gover=
nance, in which Microsoft agreed to invest $13 billion in OpenAI, and other=
 venture capitalists also put in money, and employees got stock grants, wit=
hout the contractual or fiduciary rights that investors would normally get.=
 And then one day the board of OpenAI was like =E2=80=9Chey we decided to b=
low up the company=E2=80=9D and the investors were like =E2=80=9Cwait a min=
ute can you really do that=E2=80=9D and the board was like =E2=80=9Coh yeah=
 sure we can.=E2=80=9D So <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.co=
m/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGV=
zLzIwMjMtMTEtMjAvb3BlbmFpLXN0YWZmLXRocmVhdGVuLXRvLWdvLXRvLW1pY3Jvc29mdC1pZi=
1ib2FyZC1kb2Vzbi10LXF1aXQ_Y21waWQ9QkJEMTEyMTIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1b=
T1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMxMTIxJnV0bV9jYW1wYWln=
bj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3ad1cb7f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" =
itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-dec=
oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  Bloomberg rep=
orts</a>=C2=A0that =E2=80=9Csome investors were considering writing down th=
e value of their OpenAI holdings to zero.=E2=80=9D Eighty-six billion dolla=
rs of value evaporated in a weekend.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One popular thing to say about this is that th=
e investors should have been more careful about governance, and that future=
 investors in future startups will pay more attention to things like contro=
l rights and fiduciary duties and board composition. And, maybe. But I have=
 to say I sympathize with the investors here. There are=C2=A0many cases in =
which sophisticated investors invest large sums of money into companies whe=
re they have no real control rights, and they=C2=A0<em>rationally=C2=A0</em=
>calculate that it will be fine. Generally the calculation will involve som=
e combination of factors like:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">I have met the founder and shook her =
hand and looked into her eyes and I <em>trust</em> her, so I do not need to=
 care about the corporate formalities. (Smart investors jumped into <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTAtMDMvZXZlcnlvbmUt=
d2FudGVkLXRvLWJ1eS10d2l0dGVyLXdpdGgtZWxvbj9jbXBpZD1CQkQxMTIxMjNfTU9ORVlTVFV=
GRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzExMj=
EmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0e0e0e0f" itempr=
op=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: =
#000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border=
-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;=
">  Elon Musk=E2=80=99s Twitter Inc. adventure</a> not because they did ext=
ensive due diligence or got a lot of control rights, but because he=E2=80=
=99s Elon Musk.)</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Regardless of the=
 formalities, the=C2=A0<em>incentives=C2=A0</em>are on my side: This compan=
y will need more money, the founder will need to sell her shares, and so ev=
en if she has the formal right to hose me she won=E2=80=99t, because that w=
ill be bad for her. (Both Adam Neumann and Travis Kalanick were forced out =
of startups that they had founded and where they had more or less total for=
mal control, because their investors told them =E2=80=9Chey look if you kee=
p your total control this is going to be a zero, whereas if you leave now y=
ou can salvage some value for yourself,=E2=80=9D and they made a rational c=
hoice.)</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The formalities are bad, s=
ure, but that=E2=80=99s the price of getting into this investment, and the =
<em>upside</em> of this investment is so huge that I am willing to take the=
 risk of getting hosed by bad governance. (Early investors in Facebook Inc.=
, now Meta Platforms Inc.,=C2=A0had very little in the way of governance ri=
ghts, Mark Zuckerberg had total control, and guess what he still does and h=
e has made those investors very rich.)</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is not hard to see how OpenAI=E2=80=99s inv=
estors could have had similar thoughts:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">They really liked Sam Altman! He is a=
 popular and well-connected figure among venture capital types. Nor, really=
, were they wrong to trust him. It=E2=80=99s just that usually startup foun=
ders have much more control of their boards than Altman did. That=C2=A0<em>=
did=C2=A0</em>turn out to be a failure of organizational due diligence by O=
penAI=E2=80=99s investors, but an understandable one.</li>=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">The incentives were just incredibly on their side. Open=
AI requires piles and piles of outside money to do its work, so it cannot r=
ationally afford to alienate investors. Microsoft, OpenAI=E2=80=99s biggest=
 investor, also provides its computing power and has a license to its techn=
ology and, after this weekend=E2=80=99s implosion, seems to be on track to =
hire most of its staff. =E2=80=9CYou can make the case that Microsoft just =
acquired OpenAI for $0 and zero risk of an antitrust lawsuit,=E2=80=9D <a h=
ref=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cH=
M6Ly9zdHJhdGVjaGVyeS5jb20vMjAyMy9vcGVuYWlzLW1pc2FsaWdubWVudC1hbmQtbWljcm9zb=
2Z0cy1nYWluLw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4806864d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" ite=
mscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decora=
tion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0000=
00; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Ben Thompson wrot=
e yesterday</a>. No rational startup would let that happen! Meanwhile Thriv=
e Capital was <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3344=
6549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cudGhlaW5mb3JtYXRpb24uY29tL2FydGljbGVzL3Rocml2ZS1=
jYXBpdGFsLXRvLWxlYWQtcHVyY2hhc2Utb2Ytb3BlbmFpLWVtcGxveWVlLXNoYXJlcy1hdC04MC=
1iaWxsaW9uLXBsdXMtdmFsdWF0aW9uP3JjPTFiZGk1aA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5791e=
7bc" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> leading the tender offer</a> to buy employee shares, and migh=
t=C2=A0have thought =E2=80=9Cthese employees need liquidity and will not bi=
te the hand that is feeding them.=E2=80=9D These were all, I think, very re=
asonable things to think; they were just flummoxed by a board that=C2=A0<em=
>did not act in the economic best interests of the company</em>. Again: bec=
ause it wasn=E2=80=99t supposed to! Still a jarring surprise.</li>=09<li st=
yle=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The upside was really big. I mean the company w=
as worth more than $80 billion last week, not because it was profitable (it=
 was a money pit) but because, you know, it had an <a href=3D"https://link.=
mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZX=
JnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMTEtMDkvYWRhbS1uZXVtYW5uLWlzLXNvLWdvb=
2QtYXQtdGhpcz9jbXBpZD1CQkQxMTIxMjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0=
bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzExMjEmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R=
1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBde4ea1b7" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  8% chance of being a tril=
lion-dollar company</a>. You=E2=80=99d take some governance risk for that u=
pside.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I feel like the lesson here is not so much =E2=
=80=9Cdon=E2=80=99t invest in startups without vetting the board and ideall=
y getting a board seat for yourself,=E2=80=9D and more =E2=80=9Cdon=E2=80=
=99t invest in nonprofits at an $86 billion valuation.=E2=80=9D Which I thi=
nk has never come up before? Like as far as I can tell no one in human hist=
ory has ever purchased shares in a nonprofit at an $86 billion valuation? B=
ecause purchasing shares in a nonprofit, at any valuation, is not a coheren=
t thing to do? But then OpenAI=C2=A0made it happen, for the first time, and=
 probably also the last.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are other oddities here that I do not re=
ally understand but want to mention as puzzles. Like, the status as of this=
 morning really does seem to be that Sam Altman has been fired by OpenAI, t=
hat he plans to go work at Microsoft (OpenAI=E2=80=99s biggest investor and=
 partner), and that he is going to take the large majority of OpenAI=E2=80=
=99s employees with him. What =E2=80=A6=C2=A0what do=C2=A0everyone=E2=80=99=
s contracts look like here? Like:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">When Microsoft signed its deal with Op=
enAI, did it agree to any sort of non-compete or non-solicit, like, =E2=80=
=9Cyou won=E2=80=99t hire away our employees=E2=80=9D?</li>=09<li style=3D"=
margin-bottom: 5px;">Does Altman have any sort of non-solicit, like, =E2=80=
=9Cif you leave you won=E2=80=99t hire everyone else=E2=80=9D?</li>=09<li s=
tyle=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Do the employees have any sort of non-disclosu=
re agreements, like, =E2=80=9Cif you leave you won=E2=80=99t take our propr=
ietary technology with you=E2=80=9D?=C2=A0Perhaps this one doesn=E2=80=99t =
matter, since Microsoft has a license to OpenAI=E2=80=99s intellectual prop=
erty: The OpenAI employees can leave, take nothing with them, and then go w=
ork at Microsoft and have access to everything they left behind. But Bloomb=
erg=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3344=
6549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMTE=
tMjEvbWljcm9zb2Z0LWhpcmluZy1zYW0tYWx0bWFuLXByZXNlbnRzLW5ldy1jaGFsbGVuZ2VzLW=
Zvci1jb21wYW55P2NtcGlkPUJCRDExMjEyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmd=
XRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMTEyMSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlz=
dHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBab9501b4" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  Austin Carr reports</a=
> that =E2=80=9Cany employees who do join Microsoft can=E2=80=99t simply re=
plicate the work they were doing on OpenAI properties like GPT-5 without in=
viting a nightmare of claims over trade-secret theft.=E2=80=9D</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Coming from the world of finance, all of this =
feels odd to me; ordinarily there would be contracts preventing a company=
=E2=80=99s biggest customer from hiring its CEO and him then bringing over =
his whole team to build the same cutting-edge technology they were building=
 at the company. Here, I guess, there aren=E2=80=99t? Everyone just trusted=
 each other? Seems like a mistake.</p>

<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Binance</h2>
</td>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Broadly speaking the US government has two obj=
ections to Binance Holdings Ltd.:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Binance is a crypto exchange, and the=
 US Securities and Exchange Commission thinks that crypto exchanges are mor=
e or less illegal under US law.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnot=
e-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !imp=
ortant; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a>
</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Binance, as a crypto exchange, pr=
ovides a way for people to move money internationally, and the US Departmen=
t of Justice thinks that Binance lets=C2=A0<em>bad=C2=A0</em>people move=C2=
=A0<em>bad=C2=A0</em>money to and from bad countries.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlu=
aW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDYtMDYvdGhlLXNlYy1jb21lcy1mb3ItY3J5cHRvP2NtcGlkPUJ=
CRDExMjEyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dG=
VyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMTEyMSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a3=
19a23cBe022b716" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_=
blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; =
background-color: #ccc;">  talked about</a> the first objection before, bec=
ause the SEC has actually sued Binance and laid out its objections. But it =
is, I think,=C2=A0<em>by far=C2=A0</em>the less=C2=A0serious objection, for=
 several reasons:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The SEC might be wrong on the law? Th=
ere is very much a live controversy about whether US securities regulation =
applies to crypto as broadly as the SEC thinks it does, and other crypto fi=
rms have fought the SEC in court and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/33446549.258425/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlu=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Negotiations between the Justice Department an=

said. Binance has also been under scrutiny for whether it allowed transacti=
ons that helped finance Hamas.</p>
</blockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Changpeng Zhao is scheduled to appear in Seatt=
